# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Best romance movie that will make me sick to my stomach

**Score:** 67
**Body:** I feel like all the romance movies lately have been so bad I need something gut wrenching that also I can’t stop watching pls and thank you!!! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Blue Valentine, I watched this with my partner and we didn't speak for an hour after, it just hits too close to how real love actually falls apart.
2. Portrait of a Lady on Fire
3. Last of the Mohicans 
4. age of innocence

eternal sunshine of the spotless mind

moonstruck (actually v sweet and not heart wrenching but SO GOOD)

echoing brokeback mountain, atonement, and portrait of a lady on fire for some real ugly crying
5. Atonement 
6. Somewhere in Time (1980)
7. Past Lives  
Before trilogy
  - Came here to suggest Past Lives. ❤️💔
8. Dinner in America
9. The shape of water. 

## 2. Looking for queer movies that actually feel real and not cliché

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I am trying to build a list of queer films that feel authentic and not just based on stereotypes or tragic stories.

I am not looking only for heavy “issue” films. I want movies where queer characters feel human, complex, and real, whether it is romance, coming of age, drama, or something different.

Some I already liked are Call Me By Your Name, Portrait of a Lady on Fire, and Moonlight.

I would really appreciate any recommendations, especially hidden gems or underrated films.

What is a queer movie that stayed with you after watching it?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. *Mysterious Skin* touches on some heavy issues but I think it's worth it

*Twinless*

*Port Authority*

*Pillion*

*Passages*

*Princess Cyd*

*The Miseducation of Cameron Post*

*Paris is Burning* (of course)

*Monica*

*Pimp*

*Totally F\*\*\*cked Up!*
2. Maurice (the book is great too)

Tar

I Saw the TV Glow
3. Check out Heavenly Creatures 
4. The Watermelon Woman. 
5. Pride (2014)
6. But I’m a cheerleader. It’s one of my favs and such a comfort movie. Some characters are stereotypes but I love the message and ending of the movie so much!
7. Ammonite

Beginners 
8. Imagine Me and You

Saving Face

Priscilla, Queen of the Desert
9. Brokeback Mountain?  
10. Everything, Everywhere, All at Once. The movie is fantastical but the characters are real and one of the main characters is in a queer relationship throughout the movie.

## 3. Something beautiful and not too complicated to watch stoned

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I watched Ferngully high a few weeks ago and it was ethereal. The glowing aspects of the animation, and the oil monster were so cool. Want something similar, if it's 2D animation that's even better. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Wall-E
2. Baraka
3. Flow
4. \- The Last Unicorn   
\- Flow  
\- Fantastic Mr Fox   
\- My Neighbor Totoro   
\- Fantasia 
5. Fantasia 
6. The 5th Element
7. *Across the Universe*

*Fantastic Planet*

*Princess Mononoke*

*Nausicaa of the Valley of the Wind*
8. My fav stoner films:

Stardust  
O Brother Where Art Thou  
Utena (movie version, not the tv series)  
David Attenborough documentaries  
Ever After  
The Cell 
9. Heavy Metal 
10. "Wizards" by Ralph Bakshi 78.

"Fire and Ice" by Ralph Bakshi and Frank Frazetta. Watching these 2 and the others stoned is an amazing trip.

## 4. Can someone recommend me something like Jordan Peele’s “Get Out”?

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Idk, that movie sticked with me, it felt scary in a very unexplainable way. I’m not a cinephile, but to me it’s a masterpiece. Wouldn’t say it’s my favorite movie but definitely the most unique feeling a movie ever gave me. Old Boy gave me a similar feeling, but only >!the ending!<, Get Out kept me nervous throughout the entire movie. The scene where >!the girl’s father was running the auction!< sticked with me so much, it was just unexpected and the whole unsettling (from beginning to the end) atmosphere was really peak art. Can someone suggest me something similar?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Skeleton Key (2005)
  - So underrated!
  - Yeah this movie is the first thing I thought of when I watched it… very similar.
    - Also, has the added cache of being filmed right before Katrina. It wasn't released until after. So the city itself was like a ghost.
2. Nope is by him as well and is my favorite alien concept movie maybe ever.

Obsession is going to be on similar cult classic levels as Get Out. It’s major right now.

Midsommar is a similar unsettling uncanny feeling movie where you know the people are up to something but can’t quite put your finger on it at first
  - Nope is also my favorite!
3. *Rosemary's Baby* (1968). 
Tonally the same. It's a classic. There are even some nods to this film in Get Out.


*The Stepford Wives* (1975). Similar-ish vibe. (Not the 2004 remake, though!)
4. The Wicker Man (1973)
5. Black swan 
6. Us is incredible too.  For a new one Obssession. 

## 5. suggestions for heist/crime movies?

**Score:** 13
**Body:** hii ! so im a very active movie watcher, and i really like crime, heist and action moviess! so SOME ive watched (in this genre) are:

all the 'oceans' movies (oceans 8, oceans 11, etc), triple frontier, all the rush hour movies, the gray man, the killer, rebel ridge, lift, extraction (all movies), exterritorial, land of bad, spiderman live actions, under paris, triple 9, baby driver, wheelman, army of thieves, countdown, the guilty, demon city, squad 36, bullet train explosion, mako, 47 meters down, all the 'lost bullet' movies, the courier, wrath of man, little, constantine, straw, heat, black crab, countdown, back in action, den of thieves (all parts), ambulance, ad vitam, the union

im so sorry for all that, but thats like only half LMAO

my watch list/currently watching is:

swat firefight, the rip, 21 bridges, inside man, italian man, italian job, paul blart mall cop, the 355, tower heist, the outsider, reptile, thrash, aka, uhh i cant really remember anything else lol

  
ANY SUGGESTIONSS? help a girl out

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Thief with James Caan.  If you liked Heat.
  - Soundtrack is excellent 
2. The Sting (1973) one of the OG con movies
3. If language is not a barrier for you : 

Rififi, Le cercle rouge, Topkapi
4. The Hot Rock

Sneakers 

Taking of Pellam 1 2 3
5. Heist is a great heist movie…
6. The Taking of Pelham 123 (1974 original)

The Killing (1956) early Kubrick. It's good 

M (1931). Straight banger. Doesn't feel like a 100 year old movie at all. 

Enjoy. 
7. The Italian Job. The original with Michael Caine../
8. Lucky Number Slevin
9. Heat
Den of Thieves
City of God
A Prophet
Inside Man
Triple Frontier 
Army of the Dead
Out of Sight
Reservoir Dogs
Drive 
Dead Presidents
Set it Off

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. 'I Didn't Even Know This Was Coming Out': Dwayne Johnson's Moana Live-Action Faces Brutal Box Office Flop Worse Than Jared Leto

**Score:** 3280

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Shooting a live-action, shot-for-shot version of a relatively recent animated feature is a prime example of how creatively bankrupt Disney has become.
  - I miss touchstone pictures 
  - Do Nemo live action with real fish actors, then i’m in.
2. The trailer was enough😂
  - True, the times have changed so much, just having rock himself was selling tickets, he needs to realise people have changed 
3. This movie looks like absolute dogshit and an insult to the greatness of the original movie. If it flops, that's the best thing that could happen.
  - Best part is a lot of it is CGI... So, what's the point?

HTTYD is in the same boat, but I guess it gets a pass because its live-action wasn't released within the same frigin decade.
4. I think most people have noticed they already watched these live Disney movies. You can just steam Moana. It was good. They got away with it for a while, but this model is theft. You already made the movie. Oh we'll just use live actors. The whole concept is stupid.
  - Especially stupid in this case because the "live action" remake is like 75% CGI. Now it's just in some awful uncanny valley that is strictly worse than the original art style.
    - Especially for something like the Lion King with no people in it. It's not a "live action" remake, it's just a different animation style remake.

## 2. Actors who played the same age for a very long time ?

**Score:** 641
**Body:** I’ll start - Winona Ryder played a high school senior from Heathers (1988) till Strangers With Candy (2000). If the latter doesn’t count because it’s TV and an absurdist comedy, she also played a high school senior the year prior in Girl, Interrupted (1999). Albeit for the majority of the film she is recently graduated.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Morgan Freeman has been 70 since he was 40

![gif](giphy|l4KiblqezHZTheZeE)
2. Nancy Cartwright has been 10 years old for almost 40 years now

![gif](giphy|ZJh42VKmeQ2yY)
3. Bianca Lawson literally played a high schooler for 20 years.
4. Michael Caine, he has played every character in the last 30 years, for the most part,  
![gif](giphy|nqebgv0FWfZHq)  
 rather old.
5. Bianca Lawson. She played a teenager in Saved by the Bell, Pretty Little Liars, The Vampire Diaries, and more.

https://preview.redd.it/3jhz5ckes79h1.jpeg?width=496&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=82c9b2f52c6b21cb9ad0c8bc2356d69a72943989
6. Michael J. Fox was 28 in Back to the Future II + III
7. ![gif](giphy|xT8qBnzRIMESUXqEgw)  
Regina doesn’t age 
8. I feel Jenna Ortega will be playing a highschooler for the next 20 or so years.
9. Paul Rudd
  - ![gif](giphy|wCwfoCgrCzyeI)

## 3. A24 Fans Mourn Its Death Following $75 Million Google AI Deal

**Score:** 609

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jared kushners brother joined their board about year ago. Its been coming.
  - We literally cannot have anything good in this utter shithole of a country. 
    - World\*
      - Universe\*
    - This is the lifecycle. Company starts. Becomes cool. Gets coopted by institution. Becomes lame. Dies.

And then the cycle starts again with a new company. There will be a new cool movie production company.
      - A24 value is its reputation.  Puss off fans your name means nothing. Will jump ship as soon as those directors go elsewhere
2. The audience is yearning for authenticity in the content they consume and they do this? Even the YouTube CEO is aware of how AI content diminishes their product.
3. Here's hoping this backlash will retract this deal.

With the success of Backrooms, I figured A24 would change for the worse but I didn't realize it would be so quick.
4. https://preview.redd.it/dy6h4b54m99h1.jpeg?width=183&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=998974608efd7cf6d818d003739ab3ae8eea694d
5. AI24

## 4. Creepiest scene you've seen in a NON-HORROR movie?

**Body:** DAAAAWWWWN!!! STOOOOOOOP!

1. ![gif](giphy|14c6pahskzHD2g)

## 5. Favourite Movie Subplots? (MacGruber - KFBR392)

**Score:** 175
**Body:** I know this is going to sound like I’m giving too much credit to an inane subplot in a ridiculous comedy but I can’t help but genuinely appreciate the storytelling technique in the “KFBR392” subplot in MacGruber.

It isn’t simply that I find it hilarious (I do), it’s how it functions as a character study all the way through to the payoff. MacGruber is petty, obsessive, self-centred, narcissistic and incapable of prioritizing what actually matters. It’s not that the joke keeps getting funnier each time it reappears (it does), it’s how much it tells us about Mac without unnecessary exposition.

What are your favourite subplots, funny or not, with or without payoffs?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. God damnit I seriously wrote 2 chapters of a novel with this pretty much this exact plot during COVID and didn’t realize I stole it from fucking MacGruber until this very moment 🤣
2. I don’t know if I’d call them subplots but the running gags in The Other Guys always make me chuckle. 
  - Hey don't go chasin' waterfalls
  - Dirty Mike and the Boys are revolting. LOL. 
    - We will have sex in your car again!
3. I love Tallahassee's quest for Twinkies in Zombieland.
4. I work in comics and animation, and if you ever see a license plate on a car with KFBR392, chances are I drew that car.
5. NICE CAR DICKHEAD! 
  - Fuuuck you! *KFBR392, KFBR392, KFBR392*
6. The best part is when he realizes the state of the union is going to be nuked. You think he is running away to stop that. But no, he is just in a rush now to finish destroying the car.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Close Encounters is Astonishing

**Score:** 61
**Body:** It’s been about 20 years since I watched this film….and man, were my expectations about to what expect completely misguided. I guess my schema about Spielberg is that he is the master at family-oriented, blockbuster entertainment. And while Close Encounters may have some of these qualities, it is genuinely one of the most experimental, and biggest swings, that I’ve seen in a blockbuster movie. I should have been clued into this potential experimentation with the casting of Truffaut, because in many ways, this features the same audacity as the French New Wave peeps.

Anyway, of course I’m referring to the final 40 minutes where Spielberg seems to abandon the plot (well at least the expected plot) in favour of capturing an emotion — the emotion being a sense of awe (or the sublime). This section was really pure cinema in full functioning, simply allowing music and imagery to captivate the audience without the use of dialogue. With this, all the qualities of awe and the sublime are so perfectly evoked once the grandeur nature of the ship reveals itself. The paradoxical feeling of beauty which is evoked by the sheer scope and scale of the physical ship, while also the terror of recognising how small you are in comparison with the cosmos. There is also the additional paradoxical feeling of feeling incredibly small and insignificant, while also feeling connected something much larger than yourself, and therefore, enhancing the breath of your experience. 

Furthermore, with all the metaphors pointing towards some lofty metaphysical themes, this isn’t a film that provokes a sense of existential angst about finding meaning in the universe and what reality is, but rather affirms the potential for the world having genuine meaning and us as humans being connected to something much larger in the cosmos, whether that’s God or something else. 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Glad that someone gets it.

It's a movie about meeting extraterrestrial life. That's a game changer for all of humanity. You can't just brush that off and go "space aliens" like so many movies do. People get hung up on him leaving his kids (when actually his wife leaves him) but we're talking about ADVANCED LIFE FROM OTHER WORLDS.

Close Encounters and 2001 are the only movies that seem to get this.
  - To be clear, Roy Neary does abandon his family due to his obsession and he’s portrayed from the get-go as impulsive and somewhat erratic. That’s not a criticism, just a testament to how well the character is realized through Dreyfuss’ portrayal, the script, Garr’s reactions to his behavior, the great set decoration in the house, and the kids. They were heretofore a functioning family, now at risk through Roy’s behavior. All those family scenes are comical with a genuine underpinning of a family falling apart. Garr is terrific and perfectly cast. 
    - He is losing his mind due to his encounter with the aliens and being selected by them for some special endeavor. It's basically mind control that gives him an obsessive-complusive desire to abandon everything and join the aliens. He struggles against it and it destroys his family, but he eventually succumbs and puts all his earthly desires aside, but it's not his choice and it's not willful. The movie makes clear that there are many others chosen the same way.  
    - It's a great example of how a character doesn't need to be sympathetic to be compelling.  

Roy is an ass to his family.  His obsession is devouring him and he shows not even a moment or apparent regret for what it's doing to them.  But he's so damned deep into his personal rabbit hole that we can't help but follow him down and be pulled along by his frenetic scrambling at something elusive just beyond his reach.  

And it works on us, because it's also a potent metaphor for something everyone experiences at some point, either for themselves or in someone they know.  Something comes along and takes you away from the people you love for a while, even if it's just a few hours of watching a great movie, getting absorbed in a book, playing video games, being consumed by work or hobbies.  We do it because something human in us craves that drive and the satisfaction of tracking a scent to its end, wherever it may lead and sometimes without regard to we ignored while we had our nose to the ground following it.
    - He's not impulsive or erratic. The aliens implant him with something that makes him behave in such a way. 
2. To really appreciate Close Encounters, you also have to consider the tone of alien contact and outer space movies, and sci-fi movies in general, prior to it. The ‘50s and ‘60s were loaded with monster movies and alien invasion stories, while the ‘70s had dystopian, paranoid, and generally disillusioning movies about space. By and large, sci-fi movies were horror films of one sort or another, or grim melodramas. Look at movies like 2001, Silent Running, or The Man Who Fell To Earth (or other outer space movies that would follow directly after it, like Alien, Stalker, or the Invasion of Body Snatchers remake). Look at general sci-fi like Logan’s Run, Westworld, or Soylent Green.

And then consider the structure of Close Encounters. It starts off very reminiscent of that tone, with a sense of foreboding and menace, full of paranoia and conspiracy and government coverup, yet the film is ultimately an optimistic and uplifting version of first contact, built on optimism, awe, and wonder. The takeaway was not “aliens are here, and it’s the end of the world,” it was “aliens are here, and it’s amazing.”

You cannot fully appreciate how revolutionary the film was without looking at science fiction movies in the decades leading up to it. Today, we just focus on the storytelling in a vacuum, but the general tone and worldview of the movie was a revelation in 1977, and the bait-and-switch of crafting so much of the movie to look like paranoid horror was masterful.
3. Probably his most underrated film, I would say, or at least it was until now. Spielberg has a reputation, but he's also the real deal. So much so that fans of his more mainstream work are currently deeply confused by the best thing he's made in years. 
  - It wasn't underrated then; it was a huge film.

It is now though. You never see it talked about as much as Spielberg's other films.   
I liked Disclosure Day too, but I acknowledge it's messy. So many hateposts on it on here...
    - He's my favourite director, but Disclosure Day was messy as fuck and not what the man is capable of. It was too long, parts of the plot made zero sense, the action wasn't thrilling. Yet, it still contained scenes and performance that were brilliant, and would put many other directors to shame. But to say I was disappointed is an understatement.
4. Your analysis is terrific and I just wanted to add how expertly Spielberg grounds the story with humor and the hustle and bustle of Roy’s family life with his wife & kids. As well as the motley assortment of freaks and geeks chasing the unknown from UFOs to Bigfoot. Without that anchor providing a contrast to the wonder and awe later, the film wouldn’t work nearly as well. It’s simply a terrific film. I watched it a couple weeks ago with a teen and he was completely enthralled, captivated, laughing out loud at the comedic bits, and awestruck by the finale. 

## 2. I love the neon demon

**Score:** 52
**Body:** I do  not really know why thought it's just a deeply enticing and hypnotising movie. I would say in my eyes Its one of the most enigmatic movies I have ever seen or atleast seen till this point in time. I still do not completely know what this movie was about. 

And every rewatch opens up a new interpretation is it just about yhe fashion industry?

Is it about modeling?

Is it about divine beauty and what happens when one tries to subsume it? 

Is it about the clutches of capitalism that take young people and make them into a product? 

Like I said I do not know why I love this movie it's just puzzling to me to me this is one of those things that seem like an endless puzzle like a movie with a huge thread that u can start unraveling. 

And the funniest part is I do not know if the author/director intended this at all mayhe iam asking to many questions etc. 

But the intresting part is that this is the one film where that does not matter to me. 

Do yiu have films like this or feel the same about this specific movie? 

Sorry for my writing style and sorry of this is the most unoriginal take ever or not even a take I just finally wanted to write some thoughts of mine down.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. >And the funniest part is I do not know if the author/director intended this at all mayhe iam asking to many questions etc.

Doesn't matter, what matters is what you get out of the film. So much of filmmaking is subconscious, the filmmaker is bringing all sorts of cultural and political and stylistically with them from their experience and isn't always doing so with a specific intent. 

You can never ask too many questions, the only thing to watch out for is that if you're going to make a statement about a film, make sure you have a little evidence for it at least (I used to be a TA for film studies, can you tell lol). 

But yeah, great film. Looks great, sounds great, well acted (helps that Elle Fanning is a favourite of mine). Great score. 


2. I absolutely love it, too. I remember seeing it in theater, it ending, and having no idea how I felt about it.  The more I thought about it, the more I liked it. The more I read about it, the more I liked it. I now have a film reel tattoo going down my arm with cells filled in with references to movies, or people from movies, and one of the cells is The Neon Demon. But I fully understand people not feeling the same.
3. I recently rewatched it for the first time since it came out. The music and cinematography are just as amazing 10 years later, but I was really surprised by how terrifying it is. What was very simplistic performances and writing the first couple of times I watched it all those years ago became very menacing and sinister, just like Ruby and her clique. Knowing how it ends really put into perspective every single scene and line of dialogue, especially Ruby’s question “are you food or are you sex?”. I think this movie is criminally underrated, and it’s one of NWR’s best.

## 3. Exploitation Disguised as Art: A Critique of “Blonde”

**Score:** 5
**Body:** There is only one thing I truly enjoyed in this film and that is the cinematic visuals: tone, colour, and lighting. If you  
ignore the actual narrative, it is visually appealing to an audience for its great cinematography and composition.  
The movie strives to be distinctive and eccentric through its visual aesthetics. And it is. It is artsy, but only in a way  
that will attract and maintain a specific audience entertained. The film uses shots that are not typically seen in  
everyday cinema. Using wide angle lenses, different tonalities, body cam mounts, and alternating between colour  
and monochromatic shots to create interesting composition.  
Although it is visually beautiful, the narrative and point of view in which this film was made is off-putting. It is  
nothing but an insultive and disrespectful caricature of an incredibly sad story. By the end of the movie I was  
speechless, and not in a good way. I felt numb; I simply could not wrap my head around what I had just watched.  
The best way to describe it is a setback from the progress that has been made for women in the film and  
entertainment industry. It’s perverse, tragic, and exhausting.  
It is beyond exploitative to Marilyn and her wishes; to the point where she is hyper sexualized and portrayed as  
incapable and powerless. Starting from the very standpoint of the movie title: *Blonde*. Already, the title only centres  
itself on appearances and physical attributes. It’s vague and stereotypical:  
Yes, being blonde is generally idealised as a ‘beauty norm’. But, is Marilyn truly just another blonde girl?  
From the very beginning of the movie it becomes clear to spectators that this has been created from the point of  
view of someone who wishes to diminish Marylin’s essence. Such an amazing opportunity to honour an influential  
and revolutionary woman in the entertainment industry has become a degrading almost three-hour film depicting  
every moment in her life in which men quibbled and took advantage of her being.  
They took extreme trauma and glamorised it through the male gaze. It is forceful and overtly sexual. There are  
countless scenes in which Marylin is seen topless for no reason. No woman truly lies around her house topless  
reading books, and yet Marylin seems to be an exception to this…  
In addition, Marilyn's unnecessarily addressing her romantic partners as “daddy” continues to sexualize and  
condition her under the male gaze. It is unjustified and does not serve to the overall story.  
Although most would argue that this is based on a fictional book, the film has been marketed as a biopic. Whether  
it is based on a fictional book or on real life events Marlyin still exists, and what is being portrayed on screen is not  
the life of a so-called ‘fictional character’ but of someone who actually lived. Which is something the cast and crew  
unfortunaley failed to understand.  
People will watch this to get an insight on Marilyn and who she truly was. Therefore, this film becomes misleading  
to an audience. Giving them an impression of her life that is offensive and insensitive to the woman that is the  
focal point of this film.  
Personally, I do not agree with the statement that “Blonde” is a film that strives to create awareness on the  
exploitative Hollywood culture. That is somethings that has been done before in other films, yet it has not been  
through a sexual fantasy on abuse and trauma.  
It is aching to watch how they fetishize Marilyn's sufferings.Ana de Armas' performance is at times strong. However she is consistently portraying Marilyn as ‘hysterical’  
. Although  
reaching such raw vulnerability on screen is impressive, it doesn't show character fluctuations. As a matter of fact, it  
continues to dignify Marilyn’s agonies. Eventually, the portrayal of such intense emotions throughout the entirety of the  
film becomes overwhelming and whiny.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Being that the film is loosely adapted from Joyce Carol Oates’ novel, and Oates being a horror-oriented author with a particular take on the way women are treated, my sense is that the exploitation was the point. Blonde was a tough watch, and I’ll never watch it again, but the filmmakers did a great job showing how the male gaze, and male expectations, can put a woman into a particular box regardless of her own hopes and plans for her life. The show Mad Men and, with a more modern take, The Substance are other examples. 
2. I never put my finger on this film or my issues with it, but I have long felt that it is a film that doesn't really work but nearly every critical reaction willfully flattens it in very weak readings.

This post feels consistent with the consensus when the film came out, and I just have this difficult to articulate gut feeling that the point is being missed.
3. It's a film about the horror of being seen/treated as a sex object, made by someone who doesn't seem willing to view Marilyn as anything other than a sex object.

It's similar to the age old issue with satire: without careful intention and a deft hand, satire of a thing ends up being just another example of the thing
  - See, this is a critique I can get behind. While I do like the film, I do agree that the director does get in the way of himself. It keeps it from being as hard hitting as the novel was. I really wish someone like Mary Harron would have directed this instead of Dominik. 
  - >It's similar to the age old issue with satire: without careful intention and a deft hand, satire of a thing ends up being just another example of the thing.

This perfectly describes the issue I had with The Substance. 
4. I don’t think the movie works at all, because it’s too slow and ponderous to be as light on character detail as it is, and some of the literary abstractions don’t work at all (the movie did not need a CGI fetus. If there was no better way to implement that aspect from the book, cut it). 

BUT… I think it’s bad and disingenuous when the main line of criticism of the movie revolves around “it’s not very feminist to treat Marilyn Monroe like a sex object”. The movie is adapted from a very good, award winning novel, written by a woman, explicitly about the way Marilyn’s existence was distorted into a sex object by the people around her. Was Andrew Dominik the right person to tackle a very challenging Joyce Carol Oates novel, probably not given the resulting movie, but this criticism (like a lot of the criticism I’ve seen of Blonde) is basically being offended on a conceptual level that someone had the gall to write such a book and missing the forest for the trees that it’s trying to say something about how women are perceived. 
5. Totally disagree, and o would go so far as to say you entirely missed the point of the film and the Joyce Carol Oates book it adapts. Sorry you didn’t like it but all this word vomit is entirely unnecessary and wrong headed.
6. I’m not going to endorse everything about the movie—it does seem like the director was a bit of a pig—but many of your critiques seem wrongheaded to me, and grounded in the flawed premise that there’s something inherently wrong with portraying an actress who is arguably still the most recognizable sex symbol as a sexual being.

\>she is hyper sexualized 

It would be deeply misleading to make a movie about Marilyn Monroe that pretended that she wasn’t hyper sexualized. She was considered a sex symbol as soon as she became famous and many of her roles revolve around sexuality.

\>Starting from the very standpoint of the movie title: Blonde. Already, the title only centres itself on appearances and physical attributes. It’s vague and stereotypical: Yes, being blonde is generally idealised as a ‘beauty norm’. But, is Marilyn truly just another blonde girl?

She isn’t “just another blonde girl,” she’s the archetypal blonde. It’s a good title, because even though there are millions of blondes, Marilyn Monroe is still the primary figure that most people associate with the word.

\>Such an amazing opportunity to honour an influential and revolutionary woman in the entertainment industry has become a degrading almost three-hour film depicting every moment in her life in which men quibbled and took advantage of her being.

Films need to be about more than simply honoring a subject. Marilyn Monroe was mistreated by many men in her life. To omit that from a film about her would be whitewashing the abuse that she endured in favor of a fairy tale.

\>There are countless scenes in which Marylin is seen topless for no reason. No woman truly lies around her house topless reading books

That’s false. My wife is literally doing that right now.

## 4. what are some franchise starters that nobody talks about anymore ?

**Score:** 1
**Body:** I recently watched *Godzilla* (1954) for the first time, and I was struck by how well it still holds up. It made me think about how some of the earliest films in long-running franchises are often more interesting, and sometimes more formally accomplished, than they tend to be given credit for in retrospect.

When people talk about franchises, the focus is usually on later entries or the most recognisable installments. But I’m more interested in the original films that effectively launched something much larger in scope, including sequels, spin-offs, television, merchandising, and wider cultural influence.

I’m particularly thinking of films from roughly the 1940s through the mid-1970s (up to around 1975), where the “first entry” eventually developed into a broader franchise or media ecosystem over time. Obvious examples would include *Dr. No*, *Godzilla* (1954), *Planet of the Apes*, *Psycho*, *Night of the Living Dead*, *Dirty Harry*, and *Shaft*, though I’m especially interested in less frequently discussed or more forgotten cases.

I’m interested in how these films hold up today, both in terms of filmmaking and cultural impact, and whether their status as franchise starters affects how they are perceived in retrospect.

I would also appreciate recommendations for other early franchise-starting films from this period that you think are worth paying attention to, especially ones that are overlooked in contemporary discussion or have faded somewhat from view.

What are some examples you would add, and how do you think they hold up today?

The same logic applies to Dirty Harry (can anyone outside of the republican-boomer-Eastwood fan demographic even name the sequels?), Shaft, and Psycho (the concept of Psycho existing as a “franchise” and not a single Hitchcock movie that led to some random cash grabs is kind of baffling to me, to be honest). Like, is there some legion of people championing the sequels of these movies over the originals that I’ve never ran into in my life? James Bond is a franchise with ups and downs, but Psycho is as much of a Hollywood franchise as The Sting, in the sense that there’s one great film and technically a thing called The Sting 2 exists.

## 5. Obsession is about AI and I think this reading actually makes the movie even better

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I just watched the movie and have been hunting for other posts about this but it seems not a lot of people agree! so I want to write my own baby thesis on it.

First, I want to establish why I think Obsession was about AI. What clued me in initially was the inclusion of the shot of Bear and Nikki looking at an AI generated image of a happy couple (presumably them). I saw some viewers attribute it to just couple-y things that fit the time and age, but I don't think that phase was a big enough deal in our cultural history for it to be just a fun throwaway.

Nikki's initial reaction when she first gets turned is also suspicious. The awkward dialogue and strange pacing that seemed to turn some people off, is to me the signs of AI trying to adjust to fit their user. She starts off being extremely direct but that creeps Bear out, so she apologises, pivots, apologises, pivots, then cries and hallucinates that her father has cancer. Not-Nikki has only approximate knowledge of who Nikki was before, but doesn't know the nuances like her not realising Nikki wasn't close to her father at all, she just knows that humans like Bear would react with pity when faced with a friend whose close relation is ill.

The constant apologies also mirror AI conversations. And the weird behaviours like making the cat memorial or feeding the cat to Bear feel like approximations of human behaviour and flirting, and the kind of pulling your pigtails teasing that Ian mentions at the beginning but way worse because the AI is clueless. the weird hansel and gretel incest fanfic is also written in such a flowery and inhuman way that it is definitely reminiscent of AI writing.  

Not-Nikki also seems to only operate when Bear is around, kind of like how AI agents are suspended in time when you shut your computer. And she eventually even admits that she's not Nikki and she's trying to be Nikki but she can't be Nikki and she's freaking out because she's trying to follow Bear's instructions but she can't because she doesn't understand what "stop being weird" means.

To me, all of this feels very much like smoking guns but I'd love to hear any arguments for or against this reading!

The second part is that this reading of Obsession being about AI actually makes the movie stronger! I see some push back on this because it takes away from the core message of women's agency but I actually think it makes it even stronger.

imo, the point of the movie is to explore wish fulfillment - If you made a wish for love, does that make it real? (Reminded me of the last wish in Witcher lol) Comparing Nikki to an AI dehumanises her, but that's exactly how Bear wants her. And that's what the movie wants you to see as bad.

To the disgusting cowards like Bear, the ideal woman is a hot and soulless robot who cooks and cleans, has sex whenever they want, hangs on their arm in public, doesn't ever talk back and prioritises them in every single aspect, even at her own expense. Just like an AI machine, they want to type in a prompt and control everything about their partner because they cannot handle having to introspect and fix their own issues. But the point is that it's a hollow imitation.

Bear initially succeeds with his wish, he gets the dream relationship and there's a cute romcom montage. but it shows him getting gradually more bored. it's the same shit over and over because AI can't learn beyond its capacity, it can't go beyond what it thinks is the pattern of a relationship. it constantly mirrors what you want and what society thinks it should want and will always feel hollow. it also shows us he was always more into the concept of a relationship with Nikki than Nikki herself. 

Bear also consistently puts his feelings ahead of her autonomy, never makes an effort to meaningfully raise any flags about Nikki's wellbeing because he'd rather be in a relationship than not. he suspects the willow wish worked early on but never tries to do anything about it until Nikki's behaviour starts to affect him. He'd rather continue the farce because he'd prefer a robot he can control over a woman he cannot. 

He ends the movie completely and utterly unloved because the Nikki that "loved" him is not real. there is nothing real or true about a woman being forced to love a man like a machine. 

And he, on the other hand, never once showed any interest in Nikki as a human being, never interacted with her beyond the awkward small talk. The worst part is that you can contrast this with how he interacts with Sarah. When he treats a woman with respect, he can be genuinely interested in how she's doing in life and be excited for her future, and a real relationship can actually form. But instead of choosing genuine connections, he chooses a robot. and his love for Nikki is also clearly shown as fake because even in his very last conscious act, he can't even kill himself to free her, he's still a coward to the end who continues to choose the easy way out. This might be a harsh reading, because irl giving your life for another isn't that easy ofc, but I'm seeing this as an exaggerated reverse Romeo and Juliet where neither of them want to make the final sacrifice for love - Nikki because she literally cannot choose and Bear because he remains a coward.

And btw after all that, the woman is the one who must bear the consequences because he's dead and she's now regained agency but in this horrifying state. 

All in all, I think reading the AI comparisons strengthens the overall reading of the movie. It's a cautionary tale of the importance of preserving agency because agency is what leads to real, genuine connection. I hesitate to fully claim this is the reading Curry Barker intended but the shots of AI use feel so intentional that I hope at least part of it was meant to be read this way. 

I'd love to hear everyone's thoughts! I felt this all so strongly when I came out of the cinema so it's been interesting to see opposing opinions! 

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I loved Obsession and I'm happy that it's been such a big hit and people are constantly talking about it, but I don't think it fits in (at all) with the modern moviegoer's obsession with looking for clues and hints and hidden meanings. I think this movie wears absolutely all of its themes and thoughts on its sleeve and everything I've read from Curry Barker reenforces that.

If the entire movie is an allegory for AI, then that's **significantly** less impactful than it being about actual human beings, which is why the movie is so effective. Making this about AI takes out the human element and for what reason? Why is the movie better? We feel for Nikki even though she's going around killing people. Does this mean we should feel for AI?

It's a movie about humans and how fucked up and selfish humans can be. Trying to shoehorn it into an AI story actually makes it more about computers than humans, to what point?

I think some of the links you've made to AI are a bit tenuous at best, as well. 

>What clued me in initially was the inclusion of the shot of Bear and Nikki looking at an AI generated image of a happy couple (presumably them). I saw some viewers attribute it to just couple-y things that fit the time and age, but I don't think that phase was a big enough deal in our cultural history for it to be just a fun throwaway.

If you want to say that Bear using AI for the couples picture is another taste of his character/personality, sure. But, it was a split second and this movie is *so* not a puzzle box movie.

>Nikki's initial reaction when she first gets turned is also suspicious. The awkward dialogue and strange pacing that seemed to turn some people off, is to me the signs of AI trying to adjust to fit their user. She starts off being extremely direct but that creeps Bear out, so she apologises, pivots, apologises, pivots, then cries and hallucinates that her father has cancer. 

It's not suspicious at all. Her brain is in all sorts of confusion from Bear's wish, which *just* happened. She goes back and forth because her brain keeps switching back to real Nikki for a split second. As the movie goes on and she gets more adjusted, her waking up happens much more sporadically.

>the weird hansel and gretel incest fanfic is also written in such a flowery and inhuman way that it is definitely reminiscent of AI writing.

Once you believe something is about AI, you're looking for AI in every sentence. That story never struck me even an inkling as supposed to be written by AI. It's such a major stretch.

>Not-Nikki also seems to only operate when Bear is around, kind of like how AI agents are suspended in time when you shut your computer. 

But, she does operate when he's not around. She works at the store, she talks to Sarah, she gets a ride from Ian.

>but it shows him getting gradually more bored. it's the same shit over and over because AI can't learn beyond its capacity, it can't go beyond what it thinks is the pattern of a relationship.

I didn't see that in either screening I went to. When is he shown getting bored? He's trapped and scared and too much of a coward to do anything about it. At no point is anyone getting bored with a demon watching them sleep in a corner, making dead cat dioramas, killing their friends. Boredom is the last emotion I would read from that.

I don't mean to come off too harsh, but not every movie is a puzzle with hidden meaning. The fact that this movie has people talking is awesome, but in general, it's a detriment to film analysis that modern generations always look for the hidden meaning first and foremost. This movie doesn't have to be about AI (and regardless about whether you believe it's commenting on AI, it is absolutely not **about** AI). It **is** about humans and "nice guys" and objectification.
  - I get your point and agree that movies don't all have to be about hidden meaning. I don't think you absolutely have to read it as an AI allegory and I don't think it's meant to wholly be read as one. I wouldn't be surprised if Curry Barker never intended for this reading. but I'm proposing it as a secondary reading, another way to reflect on society and how some sectors of people minimise the importance of agency and genuine connection vs imagined easy shortcuts.

I explained in my post why the movie being about AI doesn't at all take away from the core concept of it being about human beings. It's not about Nikki being an actual robot like in A.I the movie. I'm talking about the idea of these men comparing a woman to a robot and how dehumanising it is. I'm looking at it in a metaphorical, literary way and not a literal, physical way.

To me the evidence is strong, but I can understand if others don't read it because it doesn't ever focus on that, because it's not meant to be the primary reading of the movie. And that's totally fine. i think we all have our own interpretations and that's what makes sharing about movies interesting.

What I meant by her not operating when Bear is around, are the scenes where she is unable to clean herself and stays at the front door while Bear is at work. I can admit, she is still able to do things outside of Bear's purview but she always returns to his side very very quickly, compared to the full day at work which if I don't remember wrongly might be after he told her to stop being weird. so I can also see it as her  trying to follow his instructions but not understanding him

And the boredom I'm referring to is in that same montage in the beginning where they're doing coupley things like watching movies and its reminiscent of a romance movie, but the scenes repeat and he's shown to be less interested like the honeymoon is over. he visually shifts her head away from him when they're watching TV. Although Nikki was acting strange from the beginning, I think it's not until after this montage where she begins to really act out properly in an obvious enough way that he has to acknowledge she's different, kind of like an AI trying to comprehend that they need to change its methods now that it's prompter seems less enthusiastic. 

Before that montage, the weirdness is big enough to know something's wrong but not big enough he has to acknowledge it. It makes him an even bigger asshole because to a normal person, day one had enough red flags for anyone to do something about Nikki's weirdness but he's willing to accept it as long as he can be with Nikki. It's only when she starts cooking his cat and standing in the corners of his room that he starts even contemplating the idea of altering his wish, not even cancelling it. So the boredom I'm talking about is before that montage and very obviously not the rest of the movie.

I also really feel the AI generated image inclusion just has to be intentional. Every scene in a movie has to be set up, lit, shot and edited. Especially on a indie movie that has limited budget and time. I just don't know what that scene would be for if not to set this up. I don't want to falsely attribute it to Curry Barker being some genius but I think reading it as an intentional signal of not just the times we live in but the themes of the movie makes the overall viewing better, whether it's intended or not. 

I don't at all disagree the movie is about nice guys and objectification. I'm just offering this alternate secondary view as another way to see how nice guys objectify women - by seeing the ideal woman as a robot.
2. Seems far fetched initially but quite an interesting take. Opens more chain of thought if you follow thru. Here's what came into my mind.
I find it so funny that everyone wants AI to be more and more closer to concious but want a complete control over it while also want it to like them however they may be that too without consequences. 
We want to use it as per our convenience and think that it benefits us but in long term it's not i.e. if we just abuse it for shortcut to our normal brain functions. Like for example.. students getting their assignments done thru AI instead of applying their own mind defeats the whole purpose of doing it. In the long run this abuse of AI remain detrimental to their own development.

  - Yeah that's another interesting way to see it! Bear only wants Nikki when she's just weird enough that they can keep it private. He doesn't care that she's not the same Nikki because it's an easy relationship since she does everything with him in mind. Like you said it's just like how people want a conscious AI capable of doing everything but that listens to your every beck and call. Bear even treats Nikki like an AI and just like tries to tell her to stop X behaviour, change to Y behaviour, etc etc constantly prompting her to change when she's not acting within his expectations 

the movie shows that it's all hollow, that a machine-like existence cannot provide the same true and real love.
